Fnrn Por-rrrcAr- Pnecrrcns CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento. CA 9-5g14_2329
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
May 6, 2009
Thomas Donovan
1542W. Longview
Stockton, California 95207
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-09-085
Dear Mr. Donovan:
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Revolving Door
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Beciuse ybur letter seekJ general
information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 This ietter is
based solely on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Ogleshy
(r975) I FPPC ops' 71.) Moreover, the Commission does not advise with respecr to past
conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already tuk"., place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective u"iionr. Finally, our advice
is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicaUitity, if
any, of other laws such as Public contract code sections 10410 or l0aii.
QUESTTON
Does the Act prohibit you from accepting employment with a private employer
and, in that capacity, working as a consultant to your-former agency?
CONCLUSION
No. However, the Act's revolving door provisions would restrict you from
engaging in certain activities, as discussed below.
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government code Sections tt I 000 through g I 0 14. All
statutory rel'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through l8gg7 of Title 2 of the california
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
I Inftlrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
fbrmal written advice. (Secrion g3l l-t; Regulation tSjZg(ctt3l. copy enclosedj.


File No. I-09-085
Page No. 2
FACTS
You were employed with the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans)
Division of Right of Way and Land Surveys (RW&LS) since October 15, 1984 and
retired on May 1,2009. Your last position with Caltrans before retirement was as a
Senior Right of Way Agent/Utility Reviewer. You were a designated employee in your
agency's conflict of interest code and filed an annual statement of economic interests
("Form 700"). You did not have the authority to determine or set policy, although you
did discuss policy issues with your supervisor, perform assigned investigations and
assemble drafts of formal written recommendations. You reviewed and made comments
on project specific contracts, though you did not approve or make any policy decisions on
these contracts. You also proof read certain types of contracts. Upon request, you
reviewed, requested review by the Legal Division, and recommended approval of non-
standard contract language in cooperative agreements between Caltrans and local public
agencies, but you did not approve or execute such agreements.
Around 2000, you participated in discussions conceming existing Freeway Master
Agreements originally executed with utility companies between 1952 andthe mid-
1960's. You participated in discussing and recommending process changes and
streamlining, which were then modified and subsequently approved by your division
chief and passed through your department's legal division before execution. The
resulting "Freeway Master Agreement" remains in use today. A local public agency is
currently in discussions with your former department at an executive level about certain
applications of the Freeway Master Agreement to projects sponsored by local public
agencies. Those discussions originally began at your supervisor's level, and you
participated in those discussions
You have submitted your resume to several prospective employers including The
Right of Way Firm. The Right of Way Firm has expressed interest in including your
resume in a bid to the local public agency that is in talks with your Caltrans department
about the Freeway Master Agreement. You told the Right of Way Firm that you did not
want your name or resume used in the bid. You are considering working for the Right of
way Firm in the future. If you choose to work for The Right of way Firm, the firm
would only contract you out for purposes of carrying out existing provisions of the
Freeway Master Agreement, as they now are or will be when changes are adopted. you
would only be involved in the carrying out of an existing contract and not on iny changes
to the contract should you work on projects on which the Right of Way Firm bids.
A]\ALYSIS
The Act has three main post-employment restrictions, generally known as the
"revolving door" provisions:
(l) Prohibition on negotiating prospective employment (Section g7407);
(2) A "one-year ban" prohibiting a former state employee from communicating
with his or her former agency to influence the agency's administrative or
legislative action, or agency acts involving the agency's action on permit,


File No. I-09-085
Page No. 3
license, grant, contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property (Section
87406); and
(3) A "permanent ban" baning a former state employee from "switching sides"
and participating in any specific proceeding between two parties thai the
employee worked on while in state service (Sections 87400-87405).
As a former Caltrans designated employee, the Act's revolving door protisions
apply to you. These laws do not prohibit you from accepting employment with any
particular private entity. lnstead, they limit your activities once you leave state service.
Negotiating Prospective Employment.
The Act and regulations contemplate that a public official may negotiate and
accept an offer of future employment before leaving his or her current state position.
However, Section 87407 is designed to ensure that the official does not us" hir o.
her state government position to make any decisions that unduly benefit the firm that may
hire or is hiring the official. Section 87407 states:
"No state administrative official, elected state officer, or designated
employee of the Legislature shall make, participate in making, or use his
or her official position to influenc€, ffiy governmental decision directly
relating to any person with whom he or she is negotiating, or has any
arrangement concerning, prospective employment.,,
A public official is considered to be "negotiating" employment "when he or she
interviews or discusses an offer of employment with an employer or his or her agent.,'
(Regulation 18747(c)(l).) An official has an "arrangement" concerning prospective
employment when he or she accepts an offer of employment. (Regulation fizql@)(Z).)
Post-Governmental Employment Provisions
One-Year Ban - The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from
appearing before or communicating with, for compensation, his or her fLrmer agerrcy for
the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discrJtionary
act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (See Section g7406;
Regulation 18746.1.)
Section 82002(a) defines administrative action as "the proposal, drafting,
development, consideration, amendment, enactment, or defeat by any state ageicy of any
rule, regulation, or other action in any ratemaking proceeding or any quasi-legislative
proceeding."


File No. I-09-085
Page No.4
Section 82037 defines legislative action as "the drafting, introduction,
consideration, modification, enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment,
report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by either house or any
committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member 
-or 
employee
of the Legislature acting in his official capacity. 'Legislative action' also means the
action of the Govemor in approving or vetoing any bill."
The one-year.ban applies to any employee of a state administrative agency who
holds a position that is designated or should be designated in the agency', 
"olnfli"t-of-
interest code. (Section 87406(dXl); Regulation 18746.1(a)(Z).)3 ihe ban applies for 12
months from the date the employee leaves state office or employment, whicl is defined
as the date the employee permanently leaves state service or takes a leave of absence.
(Regulation I 8746. l(b)( I ) and (2).)
While in effect, the one-year ban applies only when a former employee or official
is being compensated for his or her appearances or communications before 
-his 
or her
former agency on behalf of any irerson as an agent, attorney, or representative of that
person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (a).)
The one-year ban applies to "any appearance or communication made for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit,
license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property.,' (Regulation
18746.1(b)(5).) An appearance or communication is for the "pu.pos" of inflJencing" if it
is made for the "principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifyin!,
opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or proceeding.;' lRegulation 18746:.2.f An
appearance or corrmunication includes, but is not limited to, conversing by telephone or
in person, corresponding in writing or by electronic transmission, utt"nOing a meeting,
and delivering or sending any communication. (/d.)
Finally, appearances and communications are prohibited only if they are before a
state agency that the public official worked for or represented or a state agency ..which
budget, personnel, and other operations" are subject to the control of a stale agency the
public official worked for or represented. (Regulation 1g746.1(bX6).)
However, not all communications are prohibited by the one-year ban.
Appearances or communications before a former state agency employer, made as part of
"services performed to administer, implement, or fulfill the requirlmlnts of an exiiting
permit, license, grant, contract, or sale agreement may be excluded from the [one-yearl
prohibitions . . . provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding,
or revocation of any of these actions or proceedings." (Regulation 19746.1(bXs)(A);
A governmental ernployee should be designated in his or her agency's conflict-of-interest code
if the employee makes or participates in making governmental decisions thot hur. a reasonably foreseeable
material effect on any financial interest. (Section 87302.)


File No. I-09-085
Page No. 5
Quiring Advice Letter, No. 4-03-272; Hanan Advice Letter, No. I-00-209.)
Additionally, Regular ion r87 46.Z(bX I )-(4) provides that appearances or
communications are not restricted under the one-year ban, if an individual:
"(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar
public event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the
subject matter does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding;
"(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event;
"(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or
"(4) Communicates with the press."
We have also advised that a former agency official may, without violating the
one-year ban, draft proposals on a client's behalf to be submitted to the agency ro lorg u,
the former employee is not identified in connection with the client's efforts toinfluenie
administrative action. (CookAdvice Letter, No. 4-95-321i Harrison Advice Letter, No.
A-92-289.) Similarly, a former agency official may use his or her expertise to advise
clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or policies of the officiil's former agency
so long as the employee is not identified with the employer's efforts to influence the
agency. (Perry Advice Letter, No. 4-94-004.)
You were a designated employee of Caltrans. Therefore, although you would not
be prohibited from working for The Right of Way Firm or any other firm, you would be
prohibited from making appearances before or communications with Caltrans on behalf
of a new employer for a one-year period if made for the purpose of influencing
administrative or legislative action or influencing any action involving the issriance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or
purchase of goods or property.
You have not, however, presented any facts relating to a specific appearance
before or communication with Caltrans in representation of a new emptoyei or the type of
action to which the appearance or communication relates. If you r""d f,rrth"r assistance
relating to a specific appearance or communication, you should seek further advice
providing all relevant facts of a specific communication.
Permanent Ban - The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from
"switching sides" and participating, for compensation, in any specific proceeding
involving the State of California or assisting others in the proceeding ii the procJeding is
one in which the former state employee participated while employed by the state. (See
Sections 87 401-87 402; Regulation 18741. 1.)


File No. I-09-085
Page No. 6
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any judicial, quasi-judicial, or
other proceeding in which you participated while ybu served ai u rtute administrative
official. "'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other p.oce.iing' means any proceeding,
application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, contiversy,
investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a .p".ifi"
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency . . .." (Section S'Z+Ob(c).)
Additionally, an official is considered to have "participatid" in a proceeding if he or she
took part in the proceeding "personally, and subitantially through^decision,ipp.oval,
disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering udri". on a substantial basis,
investigation, or use of confidential information . . ..,' lsection g7400(d).)
The permanent-ban does not apply to a 'new' proceeding even generally in cases
w-lere the new proceeding is related to or grows out oi a prior pioceeairg in which the
official had participated. A 'new' proceeding not subjecito the p"r-*Jrt ban typically
involves different parties, a different subject matter, or diff.r".rt iactual issues from those
considered in previous proceedings." (Rrst Advice Letter, No. A-04-lg7; also see
Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the employee's former
agency in which the former employee did not participate are considir.d r"* proceedings.
(Leslie Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new contract is one that is based o, n"*
consideration and new terms, even if it involves the same parties. (FerberAdvice Letter,
No. I-99-104i Andersorc Advice Letter, No. A-98-159.) I; addition, the application,
drafting, and awarding of a contract, license, or approval is conside."A tote a proceeding
separate from the monitoring and performarc" of th" contract, license, or approral.
(Anderson, supra; Blonien Advice Letter, No. 4-g9-463.) For example, if you only
worked on the drafting and awarding of a contract while at Caltrans,ih" p"i-unent ban
only prohibits you from working on drafting and awarding of the same contract with a
new private employer. -If, however, you worked on the arafting and awarding, as well as,
the monitoring and performing of a contract with Caltrans, the-permanent bai prohibits
you from working on the contract with a new private employer.
While you have described various projects you have been involved in while at
Caltrans, to determine whether the permanent ban applies to your situation, you need to
determine if any of the actions in which you may engage on behalf of your'riew employer
involve a proceeding in which you participated whilE ernployed at Calirans. (Regulation
18741.1(aX4).) If you need additional assistance relating to a specific proceeding in
which you previously participated, you should seek further advice proridirg all relevant
facts.


File No. I-09-085
Page No. 7
lf you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322_5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
/A,rklr'// y@Lo/,\.
By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division
SKB jgl
Enclosure

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you from accepting employment with a private employer
and, in that capacity, working as a consultant to your-former agency?

CONCLUSION: No. However, the Act's revolving door provisions would restrict you from
engaging in certain activities, as discussed below.
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government code Sections tt I 000 through g I 0 14. All
statutory rel'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.I Inftlrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or

Longview Stockton, California 95207 Re Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-09-085 Dear Mr. Donovan: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the Revolving Door provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Beciuse ybur letter seekJ general information, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2 This ietter is based solely on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Ogleshy (r975) I FPPC ops' 71.) Moreover, the Commission does not advise with respecr to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already tuk"., place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective u"iionr. Finally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicaUitity, if any, of other laws such as Public contract code sections 10410 or l0aii. QUESTTON Does the Act prohibit you from accepting employment with a private employer and, in that capacity, working as a consultant to your-former agency? CONCLUSION No. However, the Act's revolving door provisions would restrict you from engaging in certain activities, as discussed below. ' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government code Sections tt I 000 through g I 0 14. All statutory rel'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulationi of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through l8gg7 of Title 2 of the california Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. unless otherwise indicated. I Inftlrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fbrmal written advice. (Secrion g3l l-t; Regulation tSjZg(ctt3l. copy enclosedj. File No. I-09-085 Page No. 2 FACTS You were employed with the California Department of Transportation (Caltrans) Division of Right of Way and Land Surveys (RW&LS) since October 15, 1984 and retired on May 1,2009. Your last position with Caltrans before retirement was as a Senior Right of Way Agent/Utility Reviewer. You were a designated employee in your agency's conflict of interest code and filed an annual statement of economic interests ("Form 700"). You did not have the authority to determine or set policy, although you did discuss policy issues with your supervisor, perform assigned investigations and assemble drafts of formal written recommendations. You reviewed and made comments on project specific contracts, though you did not approve or make any policy decisions on these contracts. You also proof read certain types of contracts. Upon request, you reviewed, requested review by the Legal Division, and recommended approval of non- standard contract language in cooperative agreements between Caltrans and local public agencies, but you did not approve or execute such agreements.
